definition: The same logic appears in multiple places, so one change requires repeated edits and can easily drift out of sync.
ruleOfThumb: If you copy logic once, plan the extraction before the third copy.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: What is the biggest risk in this billing logic?

options.text: The same pricing formula is repeated in two places, so future pricing updates can accidentally change one path but not the other
options.explanation: When tax or fee rules change, both functions must be edited in lockstep. Missing one creates inconsistent totals for invoices versus quotes.

options.text: Using reduce for subtotal is less readable than a traditional loop
options.explanation: Loop style is subjective and minor here. The bigger maintainability problem is repeated business logic.

options.text: The magic number 100 should be moved to a constant, and that alone solves the design issue
options.explanation: A constant helps readability, but duplicated structure still remains and can still drift.

options.text: Invoice and quote totals should always use different formulas for business flexibility
options.explanation: Different formulas might be valid in some products, but this snippet shows identical logic repeated without clear intent.

questions.prompt: A VAT change to 9% was applied in checkout but forgotten in reports. What design weakness allowed this bug?

options.text: Tax logic is duplicated across multiple modules, so a single policy change requires coordinated edits that are easy to miss
options.explanation: One business rule appears in three classes. A policy update should be one code change, not a hunt across UI, export, and analytics code.

options.text: The issue is missing integration tests; with enough tests, repeated logic is not a problem
options.explanation: Tests help detect drift, but they do not remove the structural cause that makes drift likely.

options.text: Each class should own its own tax percentage to stay independent from shared policy decisions
options.explanation: If all classes reflect one tax policy, independent values are a liability, not a benefit.

options.text: The methods should be static so callers can reuse them without creating objects
options.explanation: Static methods might reduce ceremony, but duplication remains unchanged.

questions.prompt: Which design is more likely to produce inconsistent behavior after a pricing rule update?

options.text: Version B — the same policy is copied into several classes, so rule updates can land unevenly
options.explanation: Version B creates multiple maintenance points for one concept. Version A centralizes policy and keeps callers aligned.

options.text: Version A — centralizing policy creates hidden coupling and should be avoided in favor of local formulas
options.explanation: Centralizing one shared rule is intentional coupling to the domain policy and improves consistency.
